📊 Data Visualization Tutorial (Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly)¶

This notebook explores how to create effective visualizations using Python’s most popular libraries: matplotlib, seaborn, and plotly.

📚 Table of Contents¶

  1. Setup and Dataset Overview
  2. Bar Chart
  3. Line Chart
  4. Scatter Plot
  5. Histogram & KDE
  6. Box Plot & Violin Plot
  7. Heatmap & Correlation
  8. Interactive Plotly Charts
  9. Final Summary

Each chart type includes:

  • 🔍 What is it?
  • ❓ When to use it?
  • 🛠 How to code it?
  • 📈 Insights from output

📘 Chapter 1: Setup and Dataset Overview¶

🎯 Goal:¶

Set up the environment and load the cleaned Superstore dataset. This dataset will be used throughout the tutorial to create insightful visualizations.

📦 Import Required Libraries¶

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import numpy as np

# Display plots inside notebook
%matplotlib inline

# Set plot styles
sns.set(style='whitegrid')
plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (10, 6)

📂 Load the Cleaned Superstore Dataset¶

🧼 Dataset Setup & Preprocessing¶

We’ll load the cleaned Superstore dataset and ensure all column names are lowercase with underscores instead of spaces or dashes for consistency.

In [2]:
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px

# Load cleaned Superstore dataset
df = pd.read_csv("superstore_cleaned.csv")

# Standardize column names
df.columns = df.columns.str.lower().str.replace(' ', '_').str.replace('-', '_')

# Convert dates
df['order_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['order_date'])
df['ship_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['ship_date'])

df.head()
Out[2]:

In [3]:
df.columns = df.columns.str.strip().str.replace(' ', '_').str.replace('-', '_').str.lower()

📊 Chapter 2: Univariate Analysis¶

🎯 Goal:¶

Explore the distribution of single variables using common univariate plots:

  • Histogram
  • Density Plot
  • Box Plot
  • Bar Plot
  • Pie Chart

Libraries: Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly Express

📈 Histogram¶

  • What: A histogram is a graphical representation of the distribution of numerical data.
  • Why: To understand the frequency distribution of a variable.
  • When to use: Analyze distribution shape, detect skewness, and identify outliers.
In [9]:
# Seaborn histogram for Sales
sns.histplot(df['sales'], bins=30, kde=False)
plt.title("Sales Distribution - Seaborn")
plt.xlabel("Sales")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()

📌 Insight: Most sales are low-value; the distribution is right-skewed.

In [10]:
# Matplotlib histogram for Profit
plt.hist(df['profit'], bins=30, color='orange', edgecolor='black')
plt.title("Profit Distribution - Matplotlib")
plt.xlabel("Profit")
plt.ylabel("Frequency")
plt.show()

📌 Insight: Profit includes both gains and losses; losses appear as a left tail.

In [11]:
# Plotly histogram for Quantity
fig = px.histogram(df, x='quantity', nbins=20, title="Quantity Distribution - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Quantity per order is mostly small, indicating few items per purchase.

🛣️ Density Plot¶

  • What: A density plot is a smoothed curve indicating the distribution of data.
  • Why: To visualize the probability distribution.
  • When to use: Compare smooth distributions and detect multimodality.
In [12]:
# Seaborn KDE for Sales
sns.kdeplot(df['sales'], fill=True)
plt.title("Sales Density - Seaborn")
plt.xlabel("Sales")
plt.show()
In [13]:
# Plotly density-like histogram for Profit
fig = px.histogram(df, x='profit', nbins=30, histnorm='density', title="Profit Density - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: The smoothed density underscores tails and any secondary peaks.

📦 Box Plot¶

  • What: A box plot shows the five-number summary (min, Q1, median, Q3, max).
  • Why: To highlight distribution spread and outliers.
  • When to use: Compare distributions across categories and detect anomalies.
In [14]:
# Seaborn box plot: Profit by Category
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='category', y='profit')
plt.title("Profit by Category - Seaborn")
plt.show()
In [15]:
# Plotly box plot: Sales by Ship Mode
fig = px.box(df, x='ship_mode', y='sales', title="Sales by Ship Mode - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Box plots reveal greater variability and outliers in Technology and First Class shipments.

📊 Bar Plot¶

  • What: A bar plot displays categorical counts or aggregates.
  • Why: To compare values across categories.
  • When to use: Show counts or summary (sum, mean) of categories.
In [16]:
# Seaborn count plot for Category
sns.countplot(data=df, x='category')
plt.title("Count of Records by Category - Seaborn")
plt.show()
In [17]:
# Matplotlib bar: Total Sales by Segment
sales_by_segment = df.groupby('segment')['sales'].sum()
sales_by_segment.plot(kind='bar', color='green')
plt.title("Total Sales by Segment - Matplotlib")
plt.ylabel("Sales")
plt.show()
In [18]:
# Plotly bar: Top 5 States by Sales
top_states = df.groupby('state')['sales'].sum().nlargest(5).reset_index()
fig = px.bar(top_states, x='state', y='sales', title="Top 5 States by Sales - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: California leads sales, followed by New York and Texas.

🥧 Pie Chart¶

  • What: A pie chart shows parts of a whole as slices.
  • Why: To illustrate percentage contribution of categories.
  • When to use: Few categories with clear part-to-whole relationships.
In [19]:
# Matplotlib pie for Segment
seg_counts = df['segment'].value_counts()
seg_counts.plot(kind='pie', autopct='%1.1f%%', startangle=90)
plt.title("Segment Distribution - Matplotlib Pie")
plt.ylabel("")
plt.show()
In [20]:
ship_counts = df['ship_mode'].value_counts().reset_index()
ship_counts
Out[20]:
ship_mode count
0 Standard Class 5968
1 Second Class 1945
2 First Class 1538
3 Same Day 543
In [21]:
# Plotly pie for Ship Mode
ship_counts = df['ship_mode'].value_counts().reset_index()
fig = px.pie(ship_counts, names='ship_mode', values='count', title="Ship Mode Distribution - Plotly Pie")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Most orders use Standard Class shipping, reflecting standard delivery preference.

🔗 Chapter 3: Bivariate Analysis¶

🎯 Goal:¶

Explore relationships between two variables using various bivariate plots:

  • Scatter Plot
  • Line Plot
  • Correlation Heatmap
  • Strip and Swarm Plots

We will use Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly Express to explore visual relationships between variables.

📌 Scatter Plot¶

  • What: A scatter plot shows the relationship between two continuous variables.
  • Why: To detect patterns, clusters, or correlations.
  • When to use: When analyzing numeric variable interactions or trend strength.
In [22]:
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='sales', y='profit')
plt.title("Sales vs Profit - Seaborn")
plt.xlabel("Sales")
plt.ylabel("Profit")
plt.show()
In [23]:
plt.scatter(df['quantity'], df['profit'], alpha=0.5, color='green')
plt.title("Quantity vs Profit - Matplotlib")
plt.xlabel("Quantity")
plt.ylabel("Profit")
plt.show()
In [24]:
fig = px.scatter(df, x='sales', y='discount', color='category',
                 title="Sales vs Discount by Category - Plotly", opacity=0.7)
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Most profitable transactions are at low discount levels. Higher discounts often lead to losses.

📈 Line Plot¶

  • What: A line plot shows trends of a variable over a sequential axis like time.
  • Why: To analyze performance and variation over time.
  • When to use: Use line plots for temporal data or comparisons across time intervals.
In [25]:
df['order_date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['order_date'])
In [26]:
df_by_date = df.groupby('order_date')['sales'].sum().reset_index()
plt.plot(df_by_date['order_date'], df_by_date['sales'])
plt.title("Sales Over Time - Matplotlib")
plt.xlabel("Order Date")
plt.ylabel("Total Sales")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [27]:
df_by_date = df.groupby('order_date')['profit'].sum().reset_index()
sns.lineplot(data=df_by_date, x='order_date', y='profit')
plt.title("Profit Over Time - Seaborn")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [28]:
df_by_date = df.groupby('order_date')['discount'].mean().reset_index()
fig = px.line(df_by_date, x='order_date', y='discount', title="Average Discount Over Time - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Sales and profits show seasonal spikes, while discount averages remain relatively flat.

🔥 Correlation Heatmap¶

  • What: A heatmap displays correlation values between numeric variables.
  • Why: To identify potential predictors or multicollinearity.
  • When to use: When exploring linear relationships among multiple numeric features.
In [29]:
corr = df[['sales', 'quantity', 'discount', 'profit']].corr()
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f')
plt.title("Correlation Heatmap - Seaborn")
plt.show()
In [30]:
fig = px.imshow(corr, text_auto=True, color_continuous_scale='RdBu_r',
                 title="Correlation Heatmap - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Discount and Profit are negatively correlated, while Quantity and Sales show weaker relationships.

🎯 Strip & Swarm Plots¶

  • What: These show individual observations for a categorical variable against a numeric variable.
  • Why: To observe spread and overlapping data points.
  • When to use: When you want to see actual data points and their distribution within categories.
In [31]:
sns.stripplot(data=df, x='segment', y='profit', jitter=True)
plt.title("Profit by Segment - Strip Plot")
plt.show()
In [32]:
sns.swarmplot(data=df.sample(500, random_state=42), x='ship_mode', y='sales', size=3)
plt.title("Sales by Ship Mode - Swarm Plot (Sampled)")
plt.show()

c:\Users\ahmad\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\seaborn\categorical.py:3399: UserWarning:

16.8% of the points cannot be placed; you may want to decrease the size of the markers or use stripplot.

c:\Users\ahmad\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\seaborn\categorical.py:3399: UserWarning:

29.4% of the points cannot be placed; you may want to decrease the size of the markers or use stripplot.

c:\Users\ahmad\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\seaborn\categorical.py:3399: UserWarning:

23.9% of the points cannot be placed; you may want to decrease the size of the markers or use stripplot.

c:\Users\ahmad\anaconda3\Lib\site-packages\seaborn\categorical.py:3399: UserWarning:

32.6% of the points cannot be placed; you may want to decrease the size of the markers or use stripplot.

📌 Insight: Swarm plots provide a clear view of data clusters and outliers for each category.

In [33]:
sns.stripplot(data=df, x='ship_mode', y='sales', jitter=True, size=3)
plt.title("Sales by Ship Mode - Strip Plot")
plt.show()
In [34]:
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='ship_mode', y='sales')
plt.title("Sales by Ship Mode - Box Plot")
plt.show()

🎯 Categorical vs Numerical¶

  • What: Compare numerical values across different categories.
  • Why: To understand how numerical metrics vary between groups.
  • When to use: When evaluating group-wise trends, e.g. profit by segment.
In [35]:
sns.barplot(data=df, x='segment', y='profit', estimator='mean', ci='sd')
plt.title("Average Profit by Segment - Seaborn Bar Plot")
plt.show()

C:\Users\ahmad\AppData\Local\Temp\ipykernel_24688\3749051956.py:1: FutureWarning:



The `ci` parameter is deprecated. Use `errorbar='sd'` for the same effect.
In [36]:
sns.violinplot(data=df, x='segment', y='sales')
plt.title("Sales Distribution by Segment - Violin Plot")
plt.show()
In [37]:
fig = px.box(df, x='category', y='profit', color='category',
             title="Profit by Category - Plotly Box Plot")
fig.show()
In [38]:
sns.stripplot(data=df, x='ship_mode', y='sales', jitter=True, hue='segment')
plt.title("Sales by Ship Mode and Segment - Strip Plot")
plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')
plt.show()

📌 Insight: Consumer segment shows more profit variance; standard ship mode is used across all segments.

🧩 Categorical vs Categorical¶

  • What: Show relationships between two categorical variables.
  • Why: To visualize frequency or interaction patterns.
  • When to use: For category overlap, usage, and frequency comparisons.
In [39]:
sns.countplot(data=df, x='category', hue='segment')
plt.title("Category Count by Segment")
plt.show()
In [40]:
cross = pd.crosstab(df['ship_mode'], df['segment'])
sns.heatmap(cross, annot=True, fmt='d', cmap='Blues')
plt.title("Ship Mode vs Segment - Heatmap")
plt.show()
In [41]:
grouped = df.groupby(['segment', 'ship_mode']).size().reset_index(name='count')
fig = px.bar(grouped, x='ship_mode', y='count', color='segment', barmode='group',
             title="Shipment Mode by Segment - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Standard Class is dominant across all segments. Corporate segment has higher overall shipment activity.

🔗 Chapter 4: Multivariate Analysis¶

🎯 Goal:¶

Visualize and analyze relationships involving three or more variables to uncover complex interactions and deeper insights.

We will use:

  • Colored scatter plots
  • Faceted plots
  • Pair plots
  • Heatmaps
  • Treemaps

Using Matplotlib, Seaborn, and Plotly Express

🎨 Colored Scatter Plot¶

  • What: A scatter plot with an additional variable shown via color or size.
  • Why: To visualize interactions between three numerical or mixed-type variables.
  • When to use: To detect clusters, patterns, or anomalies affected by a third variable.
In [42]:
fig = px.scatter(df, x='sales', y='profit', color='segment', size='discount',
                 title="Sales vs Profit by Segment (Size = Discount)")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Profitability varies across segments. Larger discounts often associate with losses.

🔁 Pair Plot¶

  • What: A grid of scatter plots showing all variable combinations.
  • Why: To explore all pairwise relationships at once.
  • When to use: During exploratory analysis to screen for trends or correlations.
In [43]:
sns.pairplot(df[['sales', 'quantity', 'discount', 'profit']], corner=True)
plt.suptitle("Pairwise Relationships - Seaborn PairPlot", y=1.02)
plt.show()

📌 Insight: Sales and Profit have wide variance; Discount shows negative trends with Profit.

🧩 Faceted Plot¶

  • What: A set of subplots created for each level of a categorical variable.
  • Why: To compare distributions or trends across subgroups.
  • When to use: When comparing the same plot across multiple categories.
In [44]:
g = sns.FacetGrid(df, col="segment", height=4)
g.map_dataframe(sns.scatterplot, x="sales", y="profit")
g.fig.suptitle("Sales vs Profit by Segment - Seaborn Facets", y=1.05)
plt.show()
In [45]:
fig = px.scatter(df, x='sales', y='profit', facet_col='category',
                 color='ship_mode', title="Sales vs Profit Faceted by Category - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Technology has wider spread in profit/sales; furniture more tightly clustered.

🧯 Category Heatmap¶

  • What: A pivot-table-style heatmap using two categorical variables.
  • Why: To detect interaction effects between categories.
  • When to use: To find category combinations with high or low values.
In [46]:
pivot = df.pivot_table(index='segment', columns='ship_mode', values='profit', aggfunc='sum')
sns.heatmap(pivot, annot=True, fmt='.0f', cmap='YlGnBu')
plt.title("Total Profit by Segment and Ship Mode")
plt.show()
In [47]:
fig = px.imshow(pivot, text_auto=True, title="Profit by Segment and Ship Mode - Plotly")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Corporate customers using Standard Class bring highest total profit.

🌳 Treemap¶

  • What: A hierarchical visualization using nested rectangles.
  • Why: To understand part-to-whole across multiple categorical levels.
  • When to use: For high-cardinality hierarchical categories with numerical values.
In [48]:
fig = px.treemap(df, path=['category', 'sub_category'], values='sales',
                 color='profit', title="Sales & Profit by Category/Sub-Category")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Office Supplies has wide product spread. Some sub-categories have strong profit margins.

🫧 Bubble Chart¶

  • What: A scatter plot where each point has a third dimension represented by the size of the marker.
  • Why: Useful to show relationships between three continuous variables.
  • When to use: When you want to show how size varies in a two-dimensional plot.
In [49]:
import plotly.express as px
fig = px.scatter(df, x='sales', y='profit', size='quantity', color='category',
                 title="Bubble Chart: Sales vs Profit (Size = Quantity, Color = Category)")
fig.show()

📌 Insight: Technology has larger sales-profit outliers; some categories have low profit despite high sales.

In [50]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
scatter = plt.scatter(df['sales'], df['profit'], s=df['quantity']*10, alpha=0.5, c=pd.factorize(df['category'])[0])
plt.xlabel("Sales")
plt.ylabel("Profit")
plt.title("Bubble Chart with Matplotlib: Sales vs Profit (Size = Quantity)")
plt.show()

🏞️ Area Plot¶

  • What: A line plot with the area below filled with color.
  • Why: Helps visualize cumulative totals or trends over time.
  • When to use: When showing how a quantity changes cumulatively or over categories.
In [51]:
df_month = df.copy()
df_month['order_month'] = df_month['order_date'].dt.to_period('M')
area_data = df_month.groupby(['order_month', 'segment'])['sales'].sum().unstack().fillna(0)
area_data.plot.area(figsize=(10,6), cmap='Set2', title='Monthly Sales by Segment - Area Plot')
plt.ylabel("Sales")
plt.xlabel("Month")
plt.show()

📌 Insight: Consumer segment consistently dominates sales volume over time.

In [52]:
df_area = df.groupby(['order_date', 'category'])['sales'].sum().reset_index()
fig = px.area(df_area, x='order_date', y='sales', color='category', title="Sales Over Time by Category - Plotly Area Plot")
fig.show()

🧱 Subplots¶

  • What: Multiple plots displayed in a single figure layout.
  • Why: Useful for comparing distributions or trends across multiple groups.
  • When to use: When visualizing multiple relationships side-by-side for context.
In [53]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(18, 5), sharey=True)
segments = df['segment'].unique()
for i, seg in enumerate(segments):
    seg_data = df[df['segment'] == seg]
    axes[i].hist(seg_data['sales'], bins=20, color='skyblue')
    axes[i].set_title(f"{seg} Sales")
    axes[i].set_xlabel("Sales")
    axes[i].set_ylabel("Count")
plt.suptitle("Sales Distribution by Segment - Subplots")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
In [54]:
g = sns.FacetGrid(df, col="segment", col_wrap=3, height=4)
g.map_dataframe(sns.histplot, x="sales", bins=20, kde=True)
g.fig.suptitle("FacetGrid Histogram of Sales by Segment", y=1.05)
plt.show()
In [ ]:

☁️ Word Cloud¶

🔍 What is it?¶

A word cloud is a visual representation of text data, where the size of each word indicates its frequency or importance.

❓ When to use it?¶

  • To visualize dominant keywords or repeated entries
  • For quick EDA of textual features

📈 Example: Most Frequent Product Names¶

In [57]:
# !pip install wordcloud
In [58]:
from wordcloud import WordCloud
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

text = ' '.join(df['product_name'].dropna().astype(str).tolist())

wordcloud = WordCloud(width=800, height=400, background_color='white').generate(text)

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 7))
plt.imshow(wordcloud, interpolation='bilinear')
plt.axis('off')
plt.title('Word Cloud of Product Names')
plt.show()

🧱 Subplots¶

🔍 What is it?¶

Subplots allow multiple plots to be displayed in a single figure layout (grid-style).

❓ When to use it?¶

  • To compare multiple visualizations side by side
  • To save space and show relationships between metrics

📈 Example: Compare Sales and Profit by Segment¶

In [59]:
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(14, 5))

# Sales by Segment
df.groupby('segment')['sales'].sum().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[0], color='skyblue')
axes[0].set_title('Total Sales by Segment')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Sales')

# Profit by Segment
df.groupby('segment')['profit'].sum().plot(kind='bar', ax=axes[1], color='salmon')
axes[1].set_title('Total Profit by Segment')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Profit')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

📌 Final Summary¶

In this tutorial, we visualized key patterns and relationships in the Superstore dataset using:

  • 📊 Matplotlib for basic static charts
  • 🎨 Seaborn for enhanced statistical plots
  • 🌐 Plotly for interactive visuals

Data visualization is essential for:

  • Communicating insights clearly
  • Validating assumptions
  • Supporting decision making

Keep practicing by trying these charts on other datasets!
